title: Science and Violence in Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: Science and Violence in Evangelion             Quote Post by citizen_shmike » Wed Feb 02, 2011 12:20 pm There is a strong association between violence and science throughout Evangelion; a science experiment gone wrong (or right, depending on who you are) devastates half of humanity; a city built with incredible skill and technology serves as a "fortress" for all mankind; enormous amounts of resources go into breeding Evangelion's, a highly dangerous weapon if there ever was one, instead of feeding a very stricken population which is going to die anyway (so is Seele's logic, I imagine). The three characters most intimately associated with the scientific core of Gerhirn and NERV are the Agaki's and Gendo. All of these characters also show the least resistance when it comes to taking the lives of other human beings or being nasty in general (and especially nasty to each other). For this reason, when I was re-watching the End of Evangelion it struck me that although Casper did refuse to initiate the program Ritsuko installed, why did she not just shoot Rei and Gendo anyway? That would have stopped Gendo's plan just as easily, wouldn't it? It's not as if these character's have any innate moral impulses like Shinji does, who even when around the corrupting influence of the Eva technology still manages to be capable of mercy i.e. the Bardiel fight. But then I thought this scene shows how the most intelligent member of NERV, Ritsuko, is the only character not to change throughout the show; the only character that ironically does not learn. I think it is very much in Ritsuko's character to submit to Gendo's will whenever the doubts inherited from her mother overtake her, and clearly she did not think the MAGI would refuse her. I'm guessing she wanted to hear what Gendo's last words would be to the extent that she was willing to accpet her death and his plan. Gendo's last words are a lie, which is also in keeping with his character. Obviously, Ritsuko is my favorite character, but what do you guys think?? (The only non-pilot to kill an angel, what?) Paragraph breaks inserted in the most reasonable places

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Wed Feb 02, 2011 1:51 pm It's very hard to make sense of your post, please familiarize yourself with the rules: http://wiki.evageeks.org/EvaGeeks_Forum_Rules_and_Guidelines#Posting

[]:             Quote Post by Hashmal » Wed Feb 02, 2011 1:56 pm some indentations would be a nice start

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Wed Feb 02, 2011 5:07 pm Science in Evangelion has only a pragmatic purpose for the characters who are in direct control of it. It never represents the ideal but stands for the bankruptcy of Enlightenment and its values. The most crucial moment for this interpretation is the short exchange between Keel and Gendo in episode 21 about the futility of science as a window to reality and the arrogance of scientists as self-proclaimed sages. The violence in Evangelion represents the liberated, authentic man. False idols are broken, a life-affirming philosophy actualized. The fate of destruction is also the joy of rebirth.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Feb 02, 2011 5:15 pm There are a number of sentiments that lean towards Romanticism towards the end of EoE -- that sort of thing goes hand in hand with the marking of scientific technological development as tainted. Dealing an obvious detail -- shooting Gendo would only open the door to SEELE doing their thing with the Adamic assets under their control. That's why Ritsuk was after doing the Samson style bringing down the temple around everyone's ears.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Feb 02, 2011 7:01 pm It's not at all surprising that they'd be conflated, seeing as how the human/angel dichotomy says that science is the result of the fruit of knowledge, our balancing factor against the angel's S2 organs or fruit of life. Science is essentially our chosen weapon for the fated duel. inb4 "all anime is about nuking 'nagashima'"

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Feb 02, 2011 9:28 pm NemZ wrote: inb4 "all anime is about nuking 'nagashima'"

[]:             Quote Post by citizen_shmike » Tue Feb 08, 2011 5:49 pm I'm new to the series/anime in general, but I'll assume from the comments my opinion isn't as original as I thought it was. Good to know.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Feb 08, 2011 6:00 pm citizen_shmike wrote: I'm new to the series/anime in general, but I'll assume from the comments my opinion isn't as original as I thought it was. Good to know.

The three characters most intimately associated with the scientific core of Gerhirn and NERV are the Agaki's and Gendo. All of these characters also show the least resistance when it comes to taking the lives of other human beings or being nasty in general (and especially nasty to each other). For this reason, when I was re-watching the End of Evangelion it struck me that although Casper did refuse to initiate the program Ritsuko installed, why did she not just shoot Rei and Gendo anyway? It's not as if these character's have any innate moral impulses like Shinji does, who even when around the corrupting influence of the Eva technology still manages to be capable of mercy i.e. the Bardiel fight. But then I thought this scene shows how the most intelligent member of NERV, Ritsuko, is the only character not to change throughout the show; the only character that ironically does not learn.
